region_of_origin: India / Singapore (Indian-origin dish served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic omelette class; no specific masala or spiced omelette subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a masala omelette entry. Closest parent is cooked whole hen's egg product. Spice and vegetable additions are not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for masala omelette or spiced egg dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One masala omelette made from two eggs with vegetables and spices, approximately 150 g, served as a main dish or side.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, hen, whole, cooked, pan-fried with vegetables and spices — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), adapted for masala omelette composition
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for whole cooked egg and individual vegetables. Masala omelette values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN whole egg (cooked) data with minor adjustments for onion, tomato, green chilli, coriander, and oil additions typical of a masala omelette recipe. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Egg, whole, cooked, fried; NDB 01129) used as cross-reference for macro validation. Values represent a typical 2-egg masala omelette per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes and omelette analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for masala omelette. GI estimated at 30 based on plain omelette / cooked egg analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Eggs are a very low-GI food (GI ~0–30 range for egg-based dishes). The addition of onion, tomato, and green chilli contributes minimal glycemic carbohydrate. GI of 30 is a conservative upper-bound estimate consistent with egg-dominant dishes. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 4.8) / 100 = 1.4, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 3.2 × 1.5 = 4.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~465 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium contribution from added salt and spices; a single serving provides approximately 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, so portion control and reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Masala omelette is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index, high protein content supporting satiety, and minimal glycemic load per serving (~1.4), making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~370 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While eggs provide beneficial nutrients including omega-3 fatty acids and B vitamins, individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate egg consumption to align with clinical dietary guidelines (typically ≤3–4 whole eggs per week) and use heart-healthy oils such as canola or olive oil in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala omelette is a popular South Asian egg dish widely served across India and in Indian restaurants in Singapore. It is made by beating whole eggs and pan-frying them with finely chopped onion, tomato, green chillies, fresh coriander leaves, and a blend of spices typically including turmeric, cumin, and salt, cooked in oil or ghee. The result is a golden, lightly spiced omelette with a soft interior and slightly crisp edges, offering a savoury, mildly spicy flavour profile with aromatic herbal notes from the coriander. Nutritionally, masala omelette is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 175 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 13 g fat, and only 3.2 g carbohydrate, making it an excellent choice for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. Its very low glycemic index (~30) and glycemic load (~1.4 per serving) make it particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Sodium content is moderate (~310 mg per 100 g) and should be considered by individuals with hypertension. Cholesterol content is notable due to the egg yolks (~370 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for cardiovascular risk management. In Singapore, masala omelette is commonly served as a breakfast or brunch item at Indian hawker stalls and restaurants, often accompanied by roti prata, bread, or rice. It is a culturally significant dish reflecting the South Indian and North Indian culinary heritage present in Singapore's diverse food landscape.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a masala omelette?
Is masala omelette good for diabetics?
What spices are in a masala omelette?
Can I eat masala omelette if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of masala omelette?
Masala omelette vs plain omelette nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact masala omelette entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked whole egg data and USDA SR Legacy fried egg values, adjusted for the typical masala omelette ingredient composition. GI is an analogous estimate from egg-based dish data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) cooked egg data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129, Egg whole cooked fried) as cross-reference, with minor upward adjustment for oil used in frying and minor carbohydrate contribution from onion, tomato, and chilli. All -1 values for monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy egg data; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on egg-based dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for masala omelette was found in approved sources. Sodium estimate includes contribution from added salt typical of restaurant preparation (~0.5 g salt per serving). Cholesterol is high due to egg yolk content and is clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk assessment. The dish is served at Maharani Table, Boat Quay, Singapore, at SGD 13.90. Halal status is assumed based on egg-only protein source and typical Indian restaurant practice in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
